Real GDP Growth Outlook — 2026 Forecast

Cambodia 2026 real GDP growth (%) — waterfall, highest to lowest (each step = markdown from the previous source)
Source: NBC (interpolated); ADB Asian Development Outlook (Apr 2026); World Bank (Jun 2026); IMF Article IV Mission (Jul 2026)

Economic Growth Road Map — Major Shocks & Events

Timeline of key global and domestic events shaping Cambodia's growth (2020–present), with their nature and likely impact
Impact: ▼▼ High negative ▼ Negative ◆ Mixed ◇ Forward risk
2020MAR
PandemicCOVID-19 Pandemic▼▼ High negative
Border closures, a tourism standstill and factory disruptions pushed real GDP into a ~3.1% contraction in 2020 — Cambodia's first recession in over two decades. Garment orders and exports began recovering from 2021.
2020AUG
Trade policyEU "EBA" Partial Withdrawal▼ Negative
The EU suspended duty-free access on about one-fifth of Cambodia's exports (garments, footwear, travel goods) over human-rights concerns, raising tariffs into a major market and pressuring export competitiveness.
2022FEB
GeopoliticalRussia–Ukraine War▼ Negative
Triggered a global surge in energy, fertiliser and food prices, feeding imported inflation that squeezed household real incomes and lifted production costs across the economy.
2022MID
CommodityGlobal Oil-Price Shock▼ Negative
Brent crude spiked above US$120/barrel in mid-2022. As a net fuel importer, Cambodia faced higher transport, logistics and manufacturing costs; prices eased through 2023.
2022–23
FinancialGlobal Monetary Tightening◆ Mixed
Aggressive US Fed rate hikes strengthened the US dollar and tightened global financing. In a highly dollarised economy, this raised effective borrowing costs and external-funding pressure.
2022–24
ExternalChina Slowdown & Property Stress▼ Negative
Weaker Chinese growth, a property downturn and fewer outbound tourists and investors reduced a major source of Cambodian FDI, construction demand and visitor arrivals.
2023–25
DomesticDomestic Property Correction▼ Negative
A cooling real-estate and construction cycle lifted non-performing loans and slowed private credit, weighing on investment and adding financial-sector vulnerabilities.
2025APR–OCT
Trade policyUS Reciprocal Tariffs▼ Negative
The US announced steep "reciprocal" tariffs in April 2025 (initially ~49% on Cambodia). A bilateral agreement on 26 Oct 2025 set the rate at 19%, easing but not removing pressure on garment and footwear exports to Cambodia's largest market.
2025MAY–DEC
GeopoliticalCambodia–Thailand Border Conflict▼▼ High negative
Clashes flared in May, escalated to full fighting on 24 Jul (first ceasefire 28 Jul) and reignited in December before a second ceasefire on 27 Dec 2025. Border closures halted cross-border trade and fuel flows and caused tourist arrivals to fall sharply.
2025JUN–26
GeopoliticalMiddle East Conflict — Iran & Strait of Hormuz▼ Negative
A 12-day Israel–Iran air war in June 2025 was followed by a sharper escalation in early 2026, when US–Israel strikes led Iran to blockade the Strait of Hormuz — the route for about a quarter of the world's seaborne oil. Brent crude jumped roughly 10–13% to ~US$80–82/barrel. As a net fuel importer, Cambodia faces renewed imported-fuel inflation and higher transport and shipping costs.
~2029AHEAD
StructuralLDC Graduation (scheduled)◇ Forward risk
Cambodia is on track to leave Least-Developed-Country status, gradually phasing out duty-free trade preferences and concessional finance — raising the premium on export diversification and competitiveness.
Information is based on publicly available news reports and research and may contain inaccuracies. It is provided for contextual purposes only and does not represent an official assessment.

How the Overview Indicators Are Built

Methodology behind the three composite views above — Economic Health Score, Macro Balance Radar, and Macroeconomic Heatmap

Economic Health Score 0–100

A composite of six equally-weighted pillars, each scored 0–100 against a policy target, then averaged into one headline score.

Score = average of the 6 pillar scores
  • Growth — real GDP growth relative to a 7% benchmark.
  • Price stability — distance of inflation from the ~3% comfort zone.
  • External buffers — reserves (months of imports) and the current-account balance.
  • Fiscal position — the budget balance as a share of GDP.
  • Credit conditions — private-credit growth kept within a healthy 5–15% band.
  • Financial stability — the NPL ratio and bank capital adequacy (CAR).
≥82 Resilient62–81 Stable45–61 Watch<45 Stressed

Macro Balance Radar 100 = norm

Each axis shows an indicator's latest reading versus its own 5-year history, expressed as a standardised (z-score) distance from the average.

100 + 25 × (latest − 5-yr mean) ÷ std. dev.

The value is bounded 40–160. 100 = exactly the 5-year average; every 25 points equals one standard deviation. All six axes are higher-is-stronger, so a shape pushing beyond 100 signals activity running above its recent norm.

  • Axes: GDP growth, exports, credit, broad money (M2), reserves, tourism.
  • Exports and tourism use a 3-month moving average to smooth volatility.
  • Reference window: the last 60 monthly observations (~5 years).

Macroeconomic Heatmap colour scale

Shows the year-on-year change of each indicator over the last 12 months, colour-coded by how each month ranks within its own row.

t = (value − row min) ÷ (row max − row min)

Each cell's position t (0–1) is mapped red → amber → green. For indicators where lower is better (e.g. inflation), the scale is flipped so improvement always reads green.

  • Green = strongest / improving month in that row.
  • Red = weakest / deteriorating month in that row.
  • Colours are relative within each indicator, not fixed thresholds.
Method notes are indicative and reflect NBC staff conventions; they are decision-support summaries, not official ratings.
Real Sector
The Real Sector monitors domestic economic activity through key indicators of growth, inflation, production, consumption, tourism, construction, and the labour market, providing an overview of current economic conditions and growth prospects.
Labour Market Snapshot
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS). Note: CLFS = Cambodia Labour Force Survey.

National Accounts — GDP, growth and economic structure

Nominal Gross Domestic Product (NGDP)

Million USD
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS) for 2025 and earlier; 2026 NBC staff estimate.

GDP per Capita

Current Prices (USD)
Source: World Bank, World Development Indicators.

Quarterly Real GDP Growth

Year-on-Year Growth (%), Constant Prices
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS); and NBC staff calculation.

Output Gap and Potential GDP

Quarterly Real GDP vs Estimated Potential (Index, first quarter = 100); Output Gap (% of potential, right)
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS); NBC staff calculationNote: potential GDP estimated with a Hodrick–Prescott filter (λ = 1600) on quarterly interpolated real GDP; output gap = (actual − potential) ÷ potential

Expenditure Contributions to Real GDP Growth

Contributions to Real GDP Growth (Percentage Points) · GDP = C + I + G + (X − M)
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS). Note: C = household consumption; I = investment (GFCF + inventories); G = government & NPISH; X − M = net exports. NPISH = Non-Profit Institutions Serving Households; GFCF = Gross Fixed Capital Formation.

GDP Expenditure Structure (2025p)

% of GDP, Constant 2014 Prices · GDP = C + I + G + (X − M)
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS). Note: C = household consumption; I = investment (GFCF + inventories); G = government & NPISH; X − M = net exports. NPISH = Non-Profit Institutions Serving Households; GFCF = Gross Fixed Capital Formation.

Year-on-Year Growth (%), Constant 2014 Prices
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS) — GDP by Production Approach

Services Sub-sector Growth

Year-on-Year Growth (%), Constant 2014 Prices
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS) — GDP by Production Approach

Tourism Nowcast — Search Interest vs Arrivals

Worldwide Google search interest in Cambodia travel terms (index 0–100, left) against tourist arrivals growth and Angkor ticket sales growth (both % y/y, 3-month average, right)
Shift search series forward:
Note: the 0–100 line is a relative index, not a count of searches or visitors. Google scales the series so that the month with the highest search interest in the selected period equals 100, and every other month is expressed as a proportion of that peak — so 50 means half the interest of the busiest month. Because the index is rebased on its own maximum, it shows the shape and direction of search demand rather than its level, and the values change if the time span, search terms or country filter are altered. It is a behavioural signal (people researching travel), not a measure of arrivals.
Source: Google Trend
Environment & Climate Watch — satellite indicators of activity, agriculture and vegetation
Filters to Hover the ⓘ on each card for what the indicator means

NO₂ Concentration — Tropospheric Column ⓘ

Monthly mean over the selected area, 10¹⁵ molecules/cm², with 12-month moving average
💡 when the line rises, traffic, factories and fuel burning are increasing — a rough sign of busier economic activity. When it falls, activity or emissions are lower. The gold dashed line smooths out the ups and downs to show the overall trend.

NO₂ Pollution Hotspots by Province

Mean tropospheric NO₂ column over the last 12 available months
💡 darker red = more NO₂ from traffic, industry and fuel burning. The highest province over the last 12 months is —. Border provinces often read high because the satellite grid also captures pollution drifting in from neighbouring countries; quiet, forested provinces stay pale.
Satellite grid: 0.25°; provincial value = mean of grid cells in each province. Border provinces can pick up transboundary pollution.

Monthly Rainfall & Anomalies ⓘ

Monthly rainfall (mm) vs long-term monthly average (dashed). Dark blue = wet anomaly (>125% of average) · amber = dry anomaly (<75%)
💡 each bar is one month of rain. Bars above the dashed line mean wetter than usual, below it drier than usual. Dark-blue bars = unusually wet (flood risk); amber bars = unusually dry (bad for rice and hydropower).

NDVI — Vegetation Health ⓘ

Monthly mean NDVI over the selected area vs historical average for the same calendar month
💡 higher = greener, healthier crops and forests. The line naturally rises in the rainy season and dips in the dry season; if it sits below the dashed line, plants are doing worse than normal for that time of year.

Vegetation Health by Province

Mean NDVI over the last 12 available months — red (stressed) to green (healthy)
💡 green = healthy vegetation, red = stressed or bare ground. Cities like Phnom Penh look red because of buildings, not crop failure.
Satellite grid: 0.1°; provincial value = mean of grid cells in each province.

Combined View — Standardized Anomalies (z-scores)

NO₂, rainfall and NDVI on a common scale: each month is expressed as standard deviations from its own calendar-month average, so the three indicators can be compared despite different units. Bars above zero = above seasonal norm.
💡 all three indicators on one common scale — bars above zero mean "higher than normal for that month", below zero "lower than normal". Reading tip: a long run of blue bars below zero (dry) followed by green bars below zero (stressed plants) = drought developing.
▮ NO₂▮ Rainfall▮ NDVIz-scores computed per calendar month over the full sample; ±1.75σ gridlines.
Real-Time & Satellite Monitors — nighttime lights, live air traffic and trade flows

Nighttime Lights from Space — Regional Development

Annual average VIIRS nighttime-light radiance by province (nW/cm²/sr) — a satellite-based proxy for economic activity and electrification. Select a year, or compare it with the previous year to see where light intensity — and development — is advancing.

National Trend — Area-weighted mean radiance

Source: NOAA/NASA VIIRS DNB monthly composites (World Bank “Light Every Night”); zonal statistics by province: Earth Observation Atlas; boundaries: geoBoundaries (ADM1). NBC staff visualization.Note: annual value = average of monthly composites; 2024 covers Jan–May. Colour scale capped at the brightest province excluding Phnom Penh, which saturates the scale. Cloud cover and stray-light correction cause some year-to-year noise in sparsely lit provinces.

Live Air Traffic over Cambodia

Aircraft currently in and around Cambodian airspace, tracked live via ADS-B — a real-time pulse of connectivity, tourism and trade. Updates automatically every 40 seconds while this page is open.
Open Flightradar24 ↗ Connecting to live feed…
Source: airplanes.live community ADS-B network (live positions; requires internet). Coverage depends on volunteer ground receivers and may miss some flights over Cambodia.For the full commercial picture, open Flightradar24 above.

Live Trade Monitor — Sea & Air Cargo

Container ships and tankers heading to Sihanoukville Autonomous Port (live AIS) and cargo aircraft around Phnom Penh (live ADS-B) — a real-time pulse of Cambodia's goods trade. Positions stream while this page is open and are recorded locally, so you can replay recent vessel and aircraft movements.
Open MarineTraffic ↗ Connecting to AIS stream…
▮ bound for Sihanoukville▮ cargo vessel▮ tanker✈ cargo flight▮ other⚓ dashed ring = 25 km anchorage
history: recording…
Sources: aisstream.io AIS network (vessel positions, destinations, ETAs); airplanes.live ADS-B (aircraft). Cargo flights identified by cargo-airline callsign.Note: coverage depends on the volunteer receiver network. History is recorded only while this page is open.

External Sector
The External Sector Dashboard provides a comprehensive overview of Cambodia's interactions with the global economy. It presents key developments in the Balance of Payments, international trade, foreign direct investment, international reserves, and exchange rate movements to assess external stability, competitiveness, and resilience against global economic shocks.
PNG ↓

Imports (amber) outpace exports (blue), so the merchandise deficit is widening even as reserves stay ample.

Monetary Sector
The Monetary and Financial Dashboard provides a concise overview of Cambodia's monetary and financial developments through key indicators on money supply, credit, interest rates, exchange rates, and financial soundness. It supports the monitoring of financial conditions and banking sector performance while facilitating timely, evidence-based economic analysis and policy assessment.
Monetary Scorecard

Year-on-Year Inflation (%): Headline, Core, Food, and Oil-Related Inflation
Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS); NBC staff calculations.

Source: National Institute of Statistics (NIS); NBC staff calculations.

Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Velocity of Money and Money Multiplier

Income Velocity of M2 (left) and M2 Money Multiplier (right)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia; NBC staff calculationNote: velocity = annualised nominal GDP ÷ broad money (M2); money multiplier = broad money (M2) ÷ monetary base. Nominal GDP annualised from interpolated monthly GDP.

Financial Soundness Indicators (FSI)
Core Financial Soundness Indicators for Deposit Takers and the Real Estate Market (IMF FSI Framework). Frequency: quarterly through 2023, monthly from January 2024 onward. Note: from 2024, the data follow the Cambodian International Financial Reporting Standards (CIFRS).

Capital Adequacy

Regulatory Capital, Tier 1 Capital and CET1 to RWA; Tier 1 Capital to Assets (%)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Asset Quality

Nonperforming Loans to Total Gross Loans, NPLs Net of Provisions to Capital, and Provisions to NPLs (%)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Earnings and Profitability

Return on Assets (Left Axis) and Return on Equity (Right Axis), %
Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Efficiency

Interest Margin and Noninterest Expenses to Gross Income (% of Gross Income)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Liquidity

Liquid Assets to Total Assets and Short-Term Liabilities (Left Axis, %); Liquidity Coverage Ratio (Right Axis, %)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Net Open Position in Foreign Exchange

Net Open Position in Foreign Exchange to Capital (%)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia
Residential Property Price Index (RPPI)
The Residential Property Price Index tracks prices of residential properties collateralised at banks and financial institutions, compiled monthly by the National Bank of Cambodia. It is a key indicator of collateral values, household wealth and real-estate-linked credit risk.

Phnom Penh Property Price Map — by Zone

Residential property price index, year-on-year change (%) for each of the three Phnom Penh zones · Month:
Year-on-year change (%) — monthly heatmap since Jan 2021
Source: National Bank of Cambodia. Central East = Chraoy Chongvar, Russey Keo, Chbar Ampov, Doun Penh, Tuol Kouk, Prampir Meakkakra, Chamkar Mon, Boeng Keng Kang; Inner Suburb = Saensokh, Mean Chey, Pur SenChey; Outer Suburb = Praek Pnov, Kamboul, Dangkao.

Fiscal Sector
Government Finance Statistics (GFS) show how the government collects revenue, spends public funds, finances the fiscal deficit, and manages its assets and liabilities. GFS helps assess the government's fiscal position, financing needs, and debt sustainability.

Fiscal Operations

Revenue & Expenditure (−) (Million USD); Net Lending/Borrowing (right)
Source: Ministry of Economy and Finance

Central Government Operations
The Central Government Operations dashboard presents the Statement of Government Operations for budgetary central government — revenue, expense, the operating balance, net acquisition of nonfinancial assets, net lending/borrowing, and financing — converted to US dollars at the average exchange rate.

Revenue, Expenditure (−), and Net Lending/Borrowing (line) — Million USD. Expenditure = expense + net acquisition of nonfinancial assets

Economic Risk Monitor
Traffic-light assessment computed from latest data against policy-relevant thresholds

Risk Dashboard

🟢 Stable · 🟡 Watch · 🔴 High risk
Rule-based assessment computed from NBC · MEF · NIS · IMF · WB · ADB assessments; thresholds shown per row

Risk Radar

0 = low risk, 100 = high risk
Method: Each risk is assessed using a traffic-light scale of Low, Moderate (Watch), or High, based on rule-based thresholds applied to the latest available data. The thresholds are presented in the Vulnerability Table below. Each rating is converted into a score: Low = 15, Moderate = 55, and High = 90. The resulting scores are plotted on the radar chart. The thresholds are broadly aligned with NBC monitoring practices and IMF and World Bank conventions. For example, inflation above 4% is classified as Moderate (Watch), while inflation above 6% is classified as High. International reserves below three months of imports indicate High risk. These thresholds are indicative and should not be interpreted as official risk ratings.

Vulnerability Heatmap

Key indicators by quarter — colour: green favourable → red unfavourable (per-row scaling)
Source: National Bank of Cambodia

Prepared by: Statistics Department, National Bank of Cambodia
Cambodia Macroeconomic Dashboard — compiled from official sources: National Bank of Cambodia (Monetary & Financial Statistics, Balance of Payments Statistics, Financial Soundness Indicators, Residential Property Price Index), Ministry of Economy and Finance (Government Finance Statistics GFSM2014, Public Debt Statistical Bulletin), National Institute of Statistics (CPI, GDP), General Department of Customs and Excise (trade), Ministry of Tourism (arrivals). International references: IMF Article IV Mission (Jul 2026), IMF WEO (Apr 2026), World Bank Cambodia Economic Update / EAP Update (Jun 2026), ADB Asian Development Outlook (Apr 2026).
